paper_title: Semantic Motion Anchors: Bridging Motion and Meaning in Co-Speech Gestures

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Learning a shared representation between spoken text and gesture is central to co-speech gesture retrieval, synthesis, and understanding, but remains challenging for semantically meaningful gestures whose communicative intent is not captured by motion alone. Direct contrastive alignment between transcripts and continuous motion embeddings often overemphasizes low-level kinematics and misses the symbolic content of semantic gestures. We propose semantic motion anchors, natural-language abstractions of gesture motion capturing physical form and communicative intent. Our method discretizes 3D gestures into body-hand motion primitives, verbalizes them into structured descriptions, and grounds them in the transcript to provide auxiliary contrastive supervision. On BEAT2, our method improves text-to-gesture R@1 by 8.2% over a direct text-motion baseline and outperforms prior retrieval approaches on text to gesture and gesture to text retrieval directions. Beyond aggregate retrieval metrics, semantic motion anchor supervision helps retrieve gestures that are semantically meaningful for the spoken query, rather than defaulting to generic motion patterns. A downstream retrieval-augmented gesture generation study showed that users significantly preferred gestures retrieved by our approach over a retrieval-augmented generation baseline, demonstrating that semantically grounded retrieval translates to gestures that better convey communicative intent in downstream generation.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Gestures are core channel of human communication. Semantically meaningful co-speech gestures can complement or reinforce spoken content, make communication more effective, and are central to tasks such as co-speech gesture synthesis and understanding [Nyatsanga et al., 2023]. key requirement underlying these tasks is learning shared space between language and gestures that meaningfully aligns raw motion sequences with spoken language. *These authors contributed equally to this work. This author is supported by the Konrad Zuse School of Excellence in Learning and Intelligent Systems (ELIZA) through the DAAD programme Konrad Zuse Schools of Excellence in Artificial Intelligence, sponsored by the Federal Ministry of Education and Research. Preprint. Learning such space that adequately captures semantic gestures, however, remains challenging. Approaches that directly map raw motion sequences with spoken text often fail to capture higher-level semantics, instead learning averaged representations that are dominated by frequent beat gestures [Nyatsanga et al., 2023, Zhi et al., 2023, Ao et al., 2023, Liu et al., 2025, Hegde et al., 2025, Mughal et al., 2025]. This is because semantic gestures are sparse and lie in the long tail of natural human motion distributions [Nyatsanga et al., 2023], making them underrepresented despite their importance for conveying communicative intent. Consequently, retrieval-based approaches have been proposed to inject semantically relevant gestures into the generation process [Zhang et al., 2024, Mughal et al., 2025]. However, these retrieval strategies are typically based on heuristic or rule-based matching, or more recently, on learning raw motion-to-text mappings specifically for semantic gestures [Hegde et al., 2025], and thus remain limited in effectively modeling semantic gesture alignment. central challenge underlying these approaches lies in how gestures and spoken language are represented and mapped. Most existing methods learn motion embeddings under reconstructionbased objectives, which emphasize low-level kinematic features, but these are often not directly aligned with communicative intent, which is highly relevant for semantic gestures. For instance, semantic gesture conveying enumeration (first, second, third) can differ significantly in articulation across speakers, yet express the same meaning. Conversely, semantic gestures with similar motion patterns may encode entirely different intents depending on discourse context. This mismatch highlights core limitation of current approaches: they conflate similarity in physical motion with similarity in semantic meaning, making it difficult to learn representations that generalize across the sparse and diverse space of semantic gestures. In this work, we argue that semantic gesture retrieval should not rely solely on directly mapping spoken text to continuous motion space, but should be supported by semantically relevant abstraction that can better link the two modalities. To this end, we introduce semantic motion anchors: structured natural-language descriptions that re-express motion abstracted in terms of physical form and communicative intent. Here, physical form refers to gesture-relevant properties such as handedness, spatial position, motion trajectory, and hand configuration [Kipp, 2005], rather than raw frame-level joint coordinates. Communicative intent captures the gestures contextual function, such as listing, self-reference and uncertainty. Together, these anchors preserve motion aspects that matter for interpretation while reducing sensitivity to low-level kinematic variation that may be irrelevant for learning the shared space. Our approach consists of three main components: First, we train two-stream RVQ-VAE [Van Den Oord et al., 2017, Liu et al., 2024] to compress continuous 3D gesture sequences into discrete motion token, and deterministically map each primitive to structured natural-language fragment describing observable spatial and kinematic properties such as hand position, movement direction, handedness, and hand configuration taken from Kipp [2005]. Second, we use an LLM to compose these token-level descriptions with the speech transcript into semantic motion anchors for each gesture. Third, we use these anchors as auxiliary supervision in contrastive text-gesture motion retrieval training. We hypothesize that this allows the model to learn relevant details required for retrieval, i.e., gesture form and function, making the mapping between spoken language and motion more semantically grounded. On BEAT2 [Liu et al., 2024], our method improves text-to-gesture R@1 from 39.1 to 42.3, +3.2 point absolute gain, corresponding to an 8.2% relative improvement over the direct text-gesture motion baseline. In downstream retrieval-augmented gesture generation study, users significantly preferred gestures retrieved by our approach over those from RAG-Gesture Mughal et al. [2025] (72.2% vs. 27.8%, < 0.0001), demonstrating that semantically grounded retrieval translates to gestures that better match communicative intent in practice. Our contributions are: (i) We introduce semantic motion anchors for text-gesture retrieval, representing co-speech gestures through natural-language descriptions of form and intent. (ii) We propose an anchor-supervised contrastive learning framework that uses motion-token verbalization and transcript grounding to improve language-gesture alignment. (iii) We release SEMANTIX, dataset of 878 human-annotated TED and BEAT2 clips with gold form and intent descriptions for evaluating semantic gesture understanding. (iv) We demonstrate gains on BEAT2 retrieval, TEDBEAT2 cross-dataset semantic retrieval, and downstream user preference against RAG-Gesture.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: 2.1 Co-Speech Gestures Co-speech gestures are an integral part of spoken communication and convey meaning jointly with speech [McNeill, 1992, Kendon, 2004]. Gesture studies commonly distinguish representational gestures, such as iconic, metaphoric, and deictic gestures, from beat gestures, which primarily mark rhythm [McNeill, 1992, 2005]. This distinction is important for computational modeling: beat gestures are frequent and relatively well captured by speech-synchronized motion models, whereas semantic gestures are sparse, context-dependent, and often lie in the long tail of natural gesture distributions [Nyatsanga et al., 2023, Ram et al., 2025, Mughal et al., 2025]. Recent co-speech gesture generation methods notably improve naturalness and temporal alignment by modeling speech-conditioned motion, but often produce generic or beat-dominated gestures [Nyatsanga et al., 2023, Zhi et al., 2023, Ao et al., 2023]. To address this, semantics-aware approaches introduce language-motion alignment, semantic planning, or retrieval-augmented generation to produce gestures that better match discourse meaning [Ao et al., 2023, Zhi et al., 2023, Zhang et al., 2024, Mughal et al., 2025, Liu et al., 2025, Ram et al., 2025]. These methods suggest that semantic grounding is important for communicatively meaningful generation. However, because their primary focus is synthesis, retrieval is often treated as an intermediate step and implemented using rule-based or heuristic matching. In contrast, we study semantic gesture retrieval as the main task, focusing on how spoken language and gesture motion can be aligned through natural-language descriptions of gesture form and communicative intent. 2.2 Text to Motion Retrieval Text-to-motion retrieval learns shared embedding space between natural-language descriptions and motion sequences [Petrovich et al., 2023]. Existing methods such as TMR and MotionGPT are typically developed on standard human motion benchmarks, where captions directly describe the performed action, making the language-motion relation relatively literal [Guo et al., 2022, Petrovich et al., 2023, Jiang et al., 2023, Petrovich et al., 2022]. Co-speech gesture retrieval is more implicit: the transcript rarely describes the gesture, but instead provides discourse context from which the gesture function must be inferred. Thus, direct transcript-motion alignment can conflate low-level kinematic similarity with communicative similarity. JEGAL Hegde et al. [2025] is closest to our setting, as it learns gesture-language alignment for co-speech gestures through direct multimodal contrastive learning [Hegde et al., 2025]. Unlike direct contrastive alignment, our approach introduces an intermediate linguistic abstraction: gestures are verbalized into physical-form and communicative-intent anchors. This allows retrieval to be shaped by symbolic gesture content rather than only raw motion similarity.

sections.title: 3 Semantic Motion Anchor Supervision for Text-to-Gesture Retrieval
sections.content: Text-to-gesture retrieval is defined over paired samples (Xi, yi), where Xi RT is 3D gesture sequence (T frames, D=114 per-frame pose dimensions over 38 upper-body joints) and yi is the spoken transcript. Standard contrastive retrieval directly aligns Xi and yi; we additionally construct semantic motion anchor ai for each pair, generated from motion and transcript and used only during training as an auxiliary contrastive supervision. 3.1 Semantic Anchor Generation The goal of semantic motion anchor generation is to convert continuous gesture motion into compact natural-language description that captures both what the gesture looks like and what communicative role it serves in context. We generate each anchor in three steps: motion tokenization, token verbalization, and transcript-grounded reasoning. Motion Tokenization: Given gesture sequence Xi, we first compress the continuous 3D motion sequence into sequence of discrete motion tokens using two-stream RVQ-VAE [Van Den Oord et al., 2017]. We use the upper body coordinates as we are interested in hand gestures and it is split into body and hand streams, Xi = (X body , hand ) and encoded separately and quantized using separate codebooks following Liu et al. [2024]. This allows each motion sequence to be converted into set of discrete motion tokens qi = (qi1, qi2, . . . , qiN ) where each token qij = (qbody , qhand ) ij represents an 8-frame segment encoded jointly across body and hand streams. Further architecture, training details and hyperparameter tuning are provided in Appendix A.1. ij Token verbalization via Gesture Attribute Extraction: Each token qij is mapped to structured natural-language fragment dij describing its observable physical properties for both hands and body. This step is grounded in Kipp [2005]s distinction between describing the visible form of gesture and interpreting its communicative function. In our case, token verbalization performs the first step: it records what the hands and arms do, without using the transcript or inferring the gestures meaning. Following Kipp [2005]s gesture annotation dimensions, we describe each token in terms of handedness, spatial location, movement trajectory, palm orientation, and coarse hand shape. We use these dimensions as the basis for our physical-form representation and automatically derive them from 3D skeleton geometry deterministically using numerical coordinates. From the body stream, we derive body-relative hand location and movement properties wrist height, depth relative to the torso, horizontal placement, elbow bend, arm reach, and motion direction capturing where the hands are placed and how they move in gesture space. From the hand stream, we extract coarse palm orientation and hand shape, including whether the palm faces inward or outward and whether the hand is open, relaxed, curled, or pointing. These attributes are mapped to chunk-level natural-language fragment dij via deterministic template function gtemp; for example, token may be verbalized as right hand rises to chest level with an open palm. Concatenating these fragments in temporal order gives physical motion narrative for the full gesture, = concat(di1, . . . , diN ), which preserves visible gesture form at higher level of abstraction than raw joint coordinates. Full details of the geometric extraction rules are provided in Appendix A.2. Transcript-Grounded Reasoning: While the token-level motion narrative captures fine-grained physical form, it does not encode the communicative role of the gesture, which is defined relative to the spoken context. We therefore ground in yi using GPT-5.4 via four-stage structured reasoning procedure (handedness, motion, intent, verification), described below. The prompt decomposes semantic motion anchor generation into four internal checks. First, handedness: the LLM determines whether the meaningful gesture is performed by one hand or both hands, using both text cues and asymmetries in the motion narrative. Second, motion: it maps the physical motion narrative into concise spatial description, including gesture level, motion path, hand relation, palm orientation, and hand shape when supported by the motion evidence. Third, intent: it infers the communicative function of the gesture from the transcript, using among functions such as emphasis, listing, enumeration, contrast, uncertainty, self-reference, other references, discourse, temporal progression/reference, relativity, emotion, negation, quantification or symbolic depiction. Fourth, verification: it checks that the inferred handedness, motion, and intent are mutually consistent before producing the final description. The resulting semantic motion anchor is compact natural language description that jointly encodes gesture form and function, e.g., Right hand rises to chest level with open palm, emphasizing the increase described in speech. We use the terms semantic motion anchors and semantic anchors interchangeably throughout the paper. We have provided the full prompt in Appendix A.3.1 and prompt sensitivity analysis in Appendix A.4. 3.2 Anchor-supervised Contrastive Learning Figure 1 illustrates the full framework. Each training sample (Xi, yi, ai) includes semantic motion anchor decomposed into two complementary components for modality-matched supervision: aphys , describing the physical form of the gesture, and aint , describing its communicative intent. The decomposition is performed via zero-shot prompt to an LLM (Qwen3-8B Yang et al. 2025); details are in Appendix A.7. All language inputs are embedded by shared frozen text encoder gtext (Qwen3Embedding-8B; Yang et al. 2025), and the motion Xi is encoded by trainable transformer fmot. Three learned projection MLPs map all representations into shared d-dimensional retrieval space: 4 Figure 1: Overview of the proposed framework. Top: The retrieval model maps transcripts and gesture motion into shared space via contrastive learning. Bottom: Semantic motion anchor generation converts continuous 3D motion into discrete tokens, verbalizes them into physical-form descriptions via gtemp, and grounds them in the transcript using an LLM to produce semantic motion anchors used as auxiliary supervision during training. zt = πtr(gtext(yi)) , zp = πan (cid:16) gtext(aphys ) (cid:17) , zs = πan (cid:0)gtext(aint )(cid:1) , zm = πmot(fmot(Xi)) (1) with all outputs ℓ2-normalised. The transcript and anchor components share the frozen encoder gtext but use separate projectors: πtr for the transcript and single shared πan for both anchor components. Training objective. Each term denotes the symmetric InfoNCE loss with learnable temperature. The full objective combines four contrastive terms: = Ltm(zt, zm) + λp Lphys(zp, zm) + λs Lint(zs, zt) + λb Lbr(zp, zs), (2) with auxiliary weights λp, λs, λb. Ltm is the primary retrieval objective, aligning transcript queries to gesture motions. Lphys anchors the motion branch to its physical-form description, preserving visually grounded structure over speaker-specific variation. Lint anchors the transcript branch to the communicative-intent description, extracting gesture-relevant content from noisy speech context. Lbr regularizes the shared anchor space at low weight. Modality-matched supervision. Motion is supervised by descriptions of how the gesture looks, while the transcript is supervised by descriptions of what the gesture means. We route each abstraction to its corresponding modality through shared anchor projector. Training proceeds in two stages: first, we train with only Ltm to establish the retrieval space; then, we fine-tune the projections and motion encoder with the full objective, initializing πan fresh so anchor supervision acts as structured regularization rather than replacing the main retrieval task.

sections.title: 4 Evaluation of Semantic Motion Anchor Generation
sections.content: 4.1 Gold Semantic Motion Anchor Annotation We evaluate generated anchor quality through comparison with human expert annotation and automated assessment. Dataset: To support anchor quality evaluation and semantic gesture understanding, we introduce SEMANTIX, human-annotated dataset of 878 semantic gesture clips from TED Expressive and BEAT2, each paired with gold descriptions of physical form and communicative intent. To obtain these samples, we begin with the annotated gesture regions from Ram et al. [2025], which were collected on subset of 10 videos from TED Expressive Liu et al. [2022b]. Each region includes 5 the corresponding transcript segment and associated video clip of the semantic gesture. We annotate gold semantic gesture descriptions for 778 such regions from the training set following the gesture annotation schema of Kipp [2005]. Each description specifies the gestures handedness, hand shape, hand orientation, spatial position, and motion trajectory, and concludes with short phrase describing its communicative intent. To establish annotation guidelines, primary annotator first labeled 231 samples. second expert independently reviewed these descriptions and either accepted or revised each annotation. The reviewed annotations had mean word-level Levenshtein distance of 0.72 with the originals. The remaining TED samples were then annotated by the primary annotator using the finalized guidelines. We further annotated 100 semantic gesture samples from the BEAT2 dataset using the same schema. LLM-as-judge validation: To enable scalable quality assessment, we validated automated LLMbased evaluation with GPT-5.4, using the prompt in Appendix A.3.2. The model compares each generated description against gold reference and assigns separate 15 Likert scores for physical gesture similarity and communicative intent similarity. Semantic motion anchor quality: We evaluate agreement between LLM-based automatic evaluation and human judgments using Spearman rank correlation. An expert annotator rated 100 generated anchors against gold annotations, using random subset of 50 TED and 50 BEAT2 examples. Each anchor was evaluated on two 5-point Likert scales: Pose Score, measuring the accuracy of the physical gesture description from 1 = incorrect to 5 = perfect, and Intent Score, measuring the accuracy of the communicative function from 1 = wrong to 5 = correct. For TED, we observe strong correlations between LLM and human judgments for both pose (ρ = 0.887, < 0.001) and intent (ρ = 0.810, < 0.001). The LLM scores are slightly lower than the human scores on average, with mean pose scores of 3.44 for the LLM and 3.75 for the human annotator, and mean intent scores of 4.20 for the LLM and 4.48 for the human annotator. For BEAT2, we observe similarly strong correlations for pose (ρ = 0.942, < 0.001) and intent (ρ = 0.947, < 0.001). Again, mean scores assigned by LLMs are slightly lower (3.34 for pose, 4.42 for intent) than scores assigned by humans (3.77 for pose and 4.60 for intent). These results suggest that the LLM-as-judge captures relative ranking trends consistent with human evaluation on this validation subset.

sections.title: 5 Text-Gesture Retrieval
sections.content: 5.1 Retrieval Setup We train the retrieval model on BEAT2 and evaluate it on the BEAT2 test split, with TED used only for out-of-domain evaluation. Each sample contains transcript window, the corresponding 3D upperbody motion sequence, and the generated physical-form and communicative-intent anchors. BEAT2 is split into 90% training, 5% validation, and 5% test sets (Ntrain=15,395, Nval=855, Ntest=856). Model selection is based on transcript-motion MRR on the BEAT2 validation split. At inference time, for text-gesture retrieval the anchor branches are discarded, and retrieval is performed only between transcript and motion embeddings using cosine similarity over the full test gallery (N =856 candidates for BEAT2; =778 for TED). We report Recall@1, Recall@5, Recall@10, and MRR for both text-to-gesture and gesture-to-text retrieval. We compare against GestureDiffuCLIP, TMR, JEGAL, and direct text-motion contrastive (Text Contrastive in the table) baseline under the same data splits and evaluation protocol. Full implementation details are provided in the Appendix A.5. Best results are bold. 6 5.2 Main Retrieval Results Does semantic motion anchor supervision improve retrieval? Table 1 compares our method against existing approaches on BEAT2 across 7 random seed runs. Our method outperforms all baselines on both retrieval directions. Relative to the strongest prior baseline JEGAL, gesture-to-text retrieval improves by 14.2% in R@1 and 9.4% in MRR, while text-to-gesture retrieval improves by 7.6% in R@1 and 6.1% in MRR. Gains are consistent but smaller at higher recall cutoffs, with R@5 and R@10 improving by 3.56.2% across both directions, reflecting that the primary benefit of semantic motion anchor supervision is concentrated at the top rank, precisely where co-speech gesture systems must commit to single retrieval decision. Beyond aggregate metrics, the cumulative rank distribution (in Appendix A.10) confirms that this advantage is concentrated in the low-ranks. Effect of anchor content on retrieval performance: Replacing the anchor text embeddings gtext(aphys) and gtext(aint) with fixed per-sample Gaussian unit vectors partially recovers the gain over the no-anchor baseline, showing that the auxiliary contrastive structure itself provides an indomain regularization benefit. However, semantic motion anchors further improve over random targets across both R@5 and MRR (p < 0.05), indicating that meaningful anchor content adds supervision beyond regularization alone. Details and the full table are provided in Appendix A.8 and Appendix A.11. Does physical-form or intent supervision matter more? Figure 2 shows the joint sensitivity of λp (physical-form) and λs (intent) on mean MRR. Performance degrades consistently as λp increases, regardless of λs, while the intent branch remains stable across broad range of λs values. This suggests the motion branch is more susceptible to overregularization than the intent branch. Peak MRR is achieved at small λp (0.010.05) with moderate λs (0.10 0.15). Marginal sensitivity curves with one weight fixed are provided in Appendix A.6. Method No Anchor Random Anchor Gesture Text Text Gesture R@5 57.54 60. MRR 46.98 50.02 R@5 58.74 60.96 MRR 48.49 51. However, co-speech gestures are many-to-many: different gesture can still express the same communicative intent. We do this analysis on the text to gesture retrieval on the test gallery of BEAT2. We perform semantic label match rate, which measures whether the top-1 retrieved gesture shares the same intent label as the ground-truth gesture. Unlike Recall@1, this metric rewards semantic alignment: retrieving different gesture instance that expresses the same communicative intent is counted as correct. From Table 3 we see that the largest numerical gains appear in Quantification, Temporal reference, Uncertainty, and Emotion, which are categories with distinctive gestural form where intent-conditioning provides the clearest signal and rarer in the data. For more examples, refer to Table 13 in Appendix. 5.4 Cross-Dataset Generalization on TED We evaluate whether retrieval model trained only on BEAT2 transfers to the unseen TED dataset Ram et al. [2025] (N =778). We focus on text-to-gesture retrieval because this is the most common retrieval direction in downstream settings such as retrieval-augmented co-speech gesture generation. In all experiments, the query is TED transcript segment. We use two controlled gallery settings. In the first setting, TED-to-TED, the gallery also consists of TED gestures. This tests whether the BEAT2-trained model can be applied to new dataset at inference time. Since both the query and gallery come from TED, we represent TED gallery gestures using only the physical-form anchor aphys, rather than transcript-derived semantic anchors, to avoid leakage from the paired transcript. In the second setting, TED-to-BEAT2, the query comes from TED and the gallery is the BEAT2 test set. This is stronger out-of-domain retrieval setting: the transcript query and gesture gallery come from different datasets, while the retrieval model itself is trained only on BEAT2. For each gallery setting, we compare two gallery representations: raw motion embeddings and semantic anchor proxies. The motion-embedding gallery tests whether the learned BEAT2 motion space transfers directly. The anchor-proxy gallery tests whether abstracting gestures into relevant physical or communicative properties provides more transferable retrieval interface. For TED-toTED, exact transcriptgesture pairs are available, so we report R@5 and MRR. For TED-to-BEAT2, exact pairs are unavailable, we evaluate retrieval using shared semantic-label metrics (Acc@1, Hit@5, Hit@10, MRR, label nDCG@10) and semantic-context similarity with frozen Qwen3 embeddings (BestCos@5, MeanCos@10, semantic nDCG@10). Metric details are provided in Appendix A.9. Method Gesture Text Text Gesture TED-to-TED: We first evaluate retrieval within TED. Directly applying our BEAT2-trained motion encoder to TED yields near-chance performance; an expected degradation due to the severe kinematic domain gap between their underlying pose estimators (SMPL-X vs. ExPose). This bottleneck, however, isolates the robustness of our learned semantic space. To prevent transcript leakage, we represent gallery gestures using only physical-form text proxies (aphys). Bypassing the out-of-domain raw motion encoder with these aphys proxies helps partially recover the retrieval performance over the textcontrastive baseline  (Table 5)  . Win-rate reports the fraction of queries where our method outperforms each baseline. transferability stems from capturing meaningful gesture structure rather than just in-domain regularization. Full table is provided in Appendix A.12. TED-to-BEAT2: The TED-to-BEAT2 setting provides stronger test of cross-dataset semantic transfer, where TED transcript queries are retrieved from the BEAT2 test gallery. Here, replacing the BEAT2 motion gallery with gesture-anchor proxies improves over the text-contrastive baseline. It also clearly outperforms the Random Anchor baseline, showing that the gain comes from the meaningful content of the anchors rather than from using any proxy representation. Overall, this suggests that abstracting gestures into relevant properties makes the representation more transferable across datasets and useful way to represent semantic gestures. 5.5 Downstream Application: Co-speech Gesture Generation In this experiment, we evaluate retrieval quality in the setting of retrieval-augmented co-speech gesture generation. Existing RAG-based gesture generation methods, such as RAG-Gesture [Mughal et al., 2025], retrieve gesture examples through rule-based retrieval around speech/text query region and use them as guidance during generation. To demonstrate valid application of our approach in gesture synthesis, we perform perceptual user study where we replace their motion retrieval step with our learned anchor-based retrieval approach. We use our approach to retrieve gestures and compare it with gestures retrieved by RAG-Gesture Mughal et al. [2025] for the same query region and the same database. Figure 3: Mean preference (%) for retrieved gestures using our approach and RAG-Gesture. Figure 3 shows that users (N=32) found gestures retrieved by our approach as more suitable (72.2% 15.0) compared to those retrieved by RAG-Gesture (27.8 % 15.0), with the difference being significant using Wilcoxon signed-rank test (W=11.5, < 0.0001). Details regarding the setup and questions asked during the user study are in Appendix A.13.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced semantic motion anchors for text-gesture retrieval: natural-language abstractions that represent gesture motion through physical form and communicative intent. Our framework converts 3D motion into upper-body tokens, verbalizes them into structured physical descriptions, and grounds them in the transcript to generate semantic motion anchors used as auxiliary supervision. Experiments on BEAT2 show that semantic motion anchor supervision improves retrieval over direct text-motion alignment. Random-anchor controls show that auxiliary supervision contributes to in-domain gains, while their collapse in cross-dataset retrieval shows the importance of meaningful semantic anchor content. Applied to retrieval-augmented co-speech gesture generation, users significantly preferred gestures retrieved by our approach over those retrieved by RAG-Gesture suggesting using semantic motion anchors produce better gestures that the match communicative intent.

sections.title: 7 Limitations
sections.content: Our semantic motion anchors capture only subset of gesture-relevant attributes; fine-grained properties such as gesture phases and subtle finger articulation are not fully modeled. The pipeline currently consists of simple contrastive setup, future work could explore other ways to incorporate anchors. Anchor generation introduces computational overhead and use of closed-source LLM. However, this is one-time cost and used only offline to create training anchors. As the method is trained primarily on BEAT2 and TED, it may not generalize equally across cultures, languages, or demographic groups, since gesture conventions vary significantly across these dimensions.

sections.title: A Technical Appendices and Supplementary Material
sections.content: A.1 RVQ-VAE Architecture and Training Training data: The RVQ-VAE is trained on combined corpus of TED Expressive [Liu et al., 2022a] and BEAT2 [Liu et al., 2024], covering over 120 hours of co-speech motion tracking data. We select 38-joint upper-body skeletons in 3D (114 dimensions per frame), with TED Expressive joint coordinates estimated via ExPose [Choutas et al., 2020] and BEAT2 coordinates extracted from SMPL-X parameters [Pavlakos et al., 2019]. We preprocess each skeleton by centering at the neck joint, scaling to unit sphere, and rotation-aligning to the torso plane via right-up-forward coordinate frame. Finger joints are additionally translated and scaled relative to their respective wrist joints. Architecture: The model separately encodes body motion (8 joints: neck, shoulders, elbows, wrists, head) and hand articulation (30 finger joints) through independent 1D convolutional encoders with temporal downsampling factor of 8. Each stream uses three-stage residual quantization with codebook sizes (128, 128, 128) for body and (128, 64, 32) for hands. The quantized representations are decoded by shared transposed convolutional decoder that reconstructs the full skeleton sequence. Our two-stream RVQ-VAE takes as input an 8-frame skeleton snippet of 38 upper-body joints (114 dimensions per frame). The input is then split into its body stream consisting of 8 joints (neck, head, shoulders, elbows, wrists) and its hand stream (3 joints per finger & 30 in total). Each of these streams is then independently encoded by 1D convolutional encoder consisting of Conv1D layers, and combined with Group Normalization and LeakyReLU activations. Each Conv1D layer uses kernel size 4, stride 2, and padding 1. This yields downsampling of 8 frames into 1 latent vector of dimension 128 per stream. Each latent sequence is independently quantised by Residual Vector Quantiser. Our final configuration uses three residual stages with codebook sizes (128, 128, 128) for the body stream and three with sizes (128, 64, 32) for the hand stream. All codebooks are first 11 initialized via k-means clustering on encoder outputs from one training batch and updated during training using EMA updates with decay µ = 0.99 to prevent codebook collapse. Codebook entries whose EMA count falls below the reset threshold of 1.0 are replaced with randomly sampled encoder outputs from the current batch, preventing inactive codes from remaining unused. The quantized body and hand latents are concatenated along the feature dimension and passed to shared transposed convolutional decoder, which mirrors the encoder. It consists of two ConvTranspose1D layers using kernel size 4, stride 2, and padding 1, with Group Normalisation and Leaky ReLU, followed by final ConvTranspose1D layer. The decoder upsamples the concatenated latents by factor of 8 to reconstruct the full 38-joint skeleton sequence across the 8 frames. The model is trained on 8-frame chunks using stream-decoupled reconstruction objective. It is trained with the Adam optimizer using learning rate of 103. Early stopping is applied using validation loss with patience of 5 epochs. We use dataloaders with clip-level batch size of 8. Two separate decoder forward passes are performed. In the first, the hand latent is detached via stop-gradient operator so that body reconstruction error flows gradients only through the body encoder and body quantizer. In the second, the body latent is detached so that hand reconstruction error flows only through the hand stream. This decoupling prevents the numerically dominant hand joints (30 of 38) from overwhelming the body streams gradient signal. The quantization loss is commitment loss only, as codebook updates are handled implicitly by EMA. The total loss objective is: = Lbody + Lhand + Lvq. (3) We report the MPJPE values in normalized coordinate space in Table 7 for different codebook configurations. In addition to codebook configurations, we also report MPJPE values with different temporal downsampling factors. Smaller factors preserve more temporal detail but produce longer token sequences, while larger factors yield more compact tokenizations at the cost of reconstruction quality. Table 8 shows that reconstruction error increases as more frames are compressed into single token. We use downsampling factor of 8 in the main experiments as trade-off between reconstruction fidelity, compact sequence length and verbalization reliability: longer primitives are more difficult to summarize with concise textual description. A.2 Rule-based Motion Primitive Verbalization Each motion token is defined by the residual codebook indices of the body and hand streams. To verbalize each of these tokens, we first reconstruct the corresponding 8-frame skeleton sequence 12 from its body and hand codebook indices, and then apply deterministic geometric rules to extract interpretable physical attributes. Doing this across all body and hand tokens yields lookup dictionary for each combination of body and hand token indices. We construct two variants of this lookup dictionary. The first enumerates all possible joint body-hand token combinations, i.e., all combinations across the body codebooks (128 128 128) and hand codebooks (128 64 32). This produces descriptions for every complete body-hand primitive, but results in very large dictionary. The second variant constructs separate stream-wise dictionaries: for the body dictionary, all body token combinations are decoded while the hand latent is held fixed at its mean value; for the hand dictionary, all hand token combinations are decoded while the body latent is held fixed at its mean value. We use this stream-wise lookup dictionary in our experiments because it preserves the same body and hand verbalization procedure while requiring only (1283) + (128 64 32) entries instead of (1283) (128 64 32) entries. A.2.1 Body-stream Attributes All spatial attributes are computed in body-normalized coordinate frame. For each frame, we compute the shoulder width wshoulder as the Euclidean distance between the left and right shoulder joints, and use the head-to-neck distance dhead as vertical scale reference. The shoulder, chest, torso, and waist levels are estimated as yshoulder = yleft shoulder + yright shoulder , ychest = yshoulder 0.5dhead, ywaist = yshoulder 1.5dhead, ytorso = ychest + ywaist 2 . (4) (5) These landmarks are used to classify wrist height of the skeleton. For each wrist, we extract vertical level, horizontal placement, depth, elbow bend, reach, and motion direction. (8) It is labeled near-body if ρ < 0.4, mid-reach if 0.4 ρ 0.7, and extended if ρ > 0.7. Arm motion direction: Wrist displacement across the 8-frame window is used to detect motion. If both total displacement and maximum single-frame displacement are below 0.03, the arm is labeled held. Otherwise, motion is decomposed into vertical, horizontal, and depth components: rising or lowering from y, moving-inwards or moving-outwards from relative to body side, and moving-forward or moving-backward from z. When motion occurs on multiple axes, the direction labels are concatenated. A.2.2 Hand-stream Attributes For each hand, we extract palm orientation and hand shape. Palm orientation: The palm normal is estimated using the cross product of two vectors on the palm plane: the vector from pinky base to index base, and the vector from wrist to palm center, which is the mean position of the four non-thumb finger base joints: npalm = (pindex base ppinky base) (ppalm centre pwrist) (pindex base ppinky base) (ppalm centre pwrist) . (9) For the right hand, the normal is negated to account for handedness. The dominant axis of the normal determines the label: x-dominant gives facing-left or facing-right, y-dominant gives facing-up or facing-down, and z-dominant gives facing-speaker or facing-away. Left/right labels are remapped into body-relative facing-inward or facing-outward labels depending on the hand side. Finger curl and hand shape: For each finger, curl is measured as the angle between the base-to-mid and mid-to-tip segments: θ(f ) curl = arccos (cid:16) clip (cid:16) base-mid ˆv(f ) ˆv(f ) mid-tip, 1, 1 (cid:17)(cid:17) . (10) The mean curl of the four non-thumb fingers is used for classification. The pipeline first checks for index-pointing, defined as index curl below 25 and mean curl of the other three fingers above 40. Remaining cases are classified as open-flat (θ < 20), open-relaxed (θ < 35), curled (θ < 55), or fist (θ 55). All of the above extracted attributes are mapped to text using deterministic templates. Each primitive produces body description or hand description, depending on the stream. Example descriptions of body and hand primitive are shown below: "body": "Left wrist held at shoulder level (vertical), in front of torso (depth) and torso side (horizontal); elbow bent, reach mid-reach; Right wrist held waist level (vertical), in front of torso (depth) and body center (horizontal); elbow bent, reach mid-reach." "hands": "Left palm facing outward, hand shape changing from curled to open relaxed; Right palm facing inward, hand shape curled, held." During inference for every gesture sample, the primitive-level descriptions are concatenated chronologically, so 24-frame gesture yields three 8-frame verbalizations each for the body and hand streams. A.2.3 Temporal aggregation of attributes All discrete body and hand attributes are first classified independently for each frame in the reconstructed 8-frame primitive. These include wrist level, depth, horizontal placement, elbow bend, reach, palm orientation, and hand shape. To obtain sequence-level description, we use the middle-frame label as the representative state when the attribute remains unchanged. To detect changes in attributes across the sequence, we compare the labels assigned to the first and last frames. If they differ, our template reports an explicit transition, e.g., "hand shape changing from curled to open relaxed" or 14 "palm rotating from facing inward to facing outward"; otherwise, the attribute is described using its middle-frame label. This aggregation is applied separately to the left and right hands. If both hands share the same orientation, hand shape, and transition pattern, they are collapsed into single bimanual description to avoid redundancy. Otherwise, the hands are verbalized separately. A.3 LLM prompts for description generation and evaluation A.3.1 Structured Reasoning-Based Prompt for Description Generation You are an expert in gesture analysis and nonverbal communication. Your task is to generate semantic description of gesture performed by speaker during talk. This description should capture both the physical motion and the communicative intent of the gesture. Input Data You will receive: 1. gesture_text the phrase being spoken during the gesture 2. text_context additional surrounding context of the utterance 3. verbalised_motion_sequence sequence of body and hand descriptions across time. Each entry represents 8 frames of motion and includes: body: wrist positions (vertical level, depth, horizontal placement), elbow bend, and reach hands: hand shape, palm orientation, and relative hand movements Important Notes About the Data The motion capture data is imperfect and lacks fine-grained detail, especially for fingers and hand shapes. The hands verbalisations are coarse approximationsdo not over-anchor on specific finger configurations. Do not hallucinate fine finger articulation unless the speech context strongly implies symbolic gesture, e.g., pointing or counting. Focus on the overall motion trajectory, spatial relationships, and timing patterns, which are more reliably captured. Critical: The verbalisations describe both hands even when only one hand performs the meaningful gesture. You must actively determine handednessdo not default to "both hands". Your Task Generate single, concise semantic description, 12 sentences, that describes: 1. The physical motion what the hands/arms do in space 2. The communicative intent what meaning or emphasis the gesture conveys in context Stage 0: Determine Handedness (critical) The verbalisations always describe both hands, but often only one hand performs the meaningful gesture. Privately consider, in order: A) Speech-based triggers for one-hand gestures Numbers or quantity words, e.g., "one", "two", "first", "second", "single", "only" likely one-hand pointing/- counting Deixis, e.g., "this", "that", "here", "there", "it" likely one-hand pointing Contrast or alternatives, e.g., "but", "however", "or", "otherwise", "instead" likely one-hand presenting an alternative Very short, emphatic phrase, 13 words likely one-hand beat/emphasis Refers to single entity being singled out likely one-hand B) Speech-based triggers for both-hands gestures Size, extent, or magnitude, e.g., "big", "huge", "large", "small", "wide", "all", "everything", "whole" likely both hands depicting size Relationship, comparison, or two-sided concept, e.g., "between", "from to Y", "versus", "and", "together" likely both hands Containment or framing, e.g., "within", "inside", "around", "the whole" likely both hands framing Inclusive self/group reference, e.g., "we", "us", "our", "ourselves" could be both hands inward motion Listing multiple items could be both hands alternating C) Motion sequence-based signals One hand static while the other shows motion verbs the moving hand is active, one-hand gesture 15 Symmetric, coordinated motion both hands One hand near-body/rest-like while the other is extended one-hand gesture, using the extended hand Opposing directions or clear bimanual coordination, e.g., apart, together, framing both hands D) Make firm commitment If speech suggests one hand and verbalisations show asymmetry definitely one-hand If speech suggests one hand but verbalisations look symmetric still likely one-hand; trust speech over verbalisations If speech suggests both hands and verbalisations show coordination definitely both hands If unclear, prefer one-hand if the gesture could reasonably be performed with one hand Privately commit to: Handedness = one hand / both hands. If one-hand, decide which hand is more active, but prefer "one hand" without specifying left/right unless clearly evident. Stage 1: Infer Communicative Intent (from text) Privately decide: What the speaker is communicating and what they might want to emphasize or illustrate The best intent category, choosing one: emphasis, listing, enumeration, contrast, uncertainty, self-reference, other references, discourse, temporal progression/reference, relativity, emotion, negation, quantification or symbolic depiction The referent: self / other / concrete object / abstract idea / imagined space / audience / number or quantity Stage 2: Map Motion from Verbalisations Privately decide: Level: waist / chest / shoulder / head; use "chest level" by default unless clearly waist/shoulder Primary motion path: up / down / inward / outward / forward / backward / lateral sweep / arc / circular / held/static / pointing If both hands: relation, e.g., together / apart / framing / mirroring / alternating Palm orientation and hand shape only if clearly supported For symbolic gestures, such as pointing, counting, or indicating: If language implies singularity, e.g., "one", infer index-finger pointing If language includes numbers, infer finger counting configuration If language includes "this", "that", "here", or "there", infer pointing Stage 3: Draft and Verify Privately: 1. Draft 12 sentence description combining handedness, motion, and intent 2. Verify correct handedness, level, primary motion, and intent 3. If any check fails, especially handedness, revise 4. Output only the final verified description Style Requirements Begin with: "One hand. . . ", "Both hands. . . ", or rarely "The right/left hand. . . " For pointing gestures: "One hand points forward with index finger extended. . . " or "One hand points using the forefinger. . . " Use concrete motion verbs: points, moves, sweeps, pushes, pulls, rises, extends, rotates, spreads Use "chest level" as the default level, not "torso level", unless clearly waist or shoulder Include palm/hand orientation only if supported, e.g., "with palm facing up" End with communicative function: "to emphasize. . . ", "to indicate. . . ", "to depict. . . ", "to convey. . . ", "to refer to. . . " Keep it concise: 1535 words, 12 sentences Use present tense Do not mention frames, chunks, VQVAE, codes, verbalisations, or any meta-commentary Output Format Output only the final semantic description. No reasoning, no tags, no explanations, no preamble. Now privately reason through all stages, starting with handedness, verify your draft, and output only the final semantic description for this input. 16 A.3.2 LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation prompt for descriptions You evaluate gesture descriptions. Compare the generated description to the gold reference. You will receive two short texts: REFERENCE (Gold) the ground-truth semantic description of gesture. CANDIDATE (Generated) model-generated semantic description of the same gesture. Core Principle: Focus on whether the descriptions capture the same gesture type, not exact wording. PoseScore (15): Physical Gesture Similarity 5 = Same gesture: same body parts, same upper-body region, and same motion category. 4 = Very similar: same body parts and region, with only minor wording differences, e.g., "spreads apart" vs. "moves outward". 3 = Partially similar: same body parts, but different motion or level while still plausible. 2 = Related but different: different number of hands or noticeably different motion type. 1 = Completely different: opposite configuration or unrelated gesture. IntentScore (15): Communicative Function 5 = Same intent: emphasis, pointing, size depiction, self-reference, etc. 4 = Very similar intent 3 = Plausible alternative interpretation 2 = Loosely related 1 = Contradictory Equivalences: Treat the following as equivalent: "chest level" = "torso level" = "in front of body" "moves apart" = "spreads" = "separates" = "outward" "pulls inward" = "moves toward body" = "brings together" "emphasize" = "mark" = "stress" = "underscore" Important: Be lenient on motion direction differences if gesture type matches. "One hand" vs. "Both hands" is significant difference, with score of 23 maximum. Focus on semantic similarity, not lexical matching. Output: Output exactly three lines: PoseScore: [15] IntentScore: AverageScore: [15] [15] No explanation. A.4 Ablations: Semantic motion anchor Generation To assess prompt sensitivity, we compare four ways of converting the same RVQ-VAE motion narrative and transcript into semantic motion anchor: naive zero-shot prompt, an in-context prompt with gesture examples, chain-of-thought prompt that separates handedness, motion, and intent, and our structured reasoning prompt, which further enforces handedness decisions, motionintent consistency, and constraints on spatial and hand-shape evidence. We evaluate all variants using the LLM-as-judge setup from Section 4.1 on 231 TED and 100 BEAT semantic gesture samples. TED (N = 231) BEAT2 (N = 100) Weighted Avg. Prompt Naive prompt In-context learning prompt Chain-of-thought prompt Structured reasoning prompt Intent 4.1 4.1 4.2 4.1 Table 10: Prompt sensitivity analysis for semantic motion anchor generation using token-based motion narratives. Scores are LLM-as-a-judge ratings on 15 scale. Intent 4.4 4.4 4.4 4.3 Intent 4.2 4.1 4.2 4. Pose 3.3 3.3 3.4 3.2 Pose 3.0 3.1 3.0 3.4 Pose 3.1 3.2 3.1 3.3 17 The structured reasoning prompt gives the best overall pose score, while Intent Score remains high across all prompt variants. This suggests that intent is often recoverable from transcript context, whereas accurate physical-form description depends more strongly on how the prompt guides the model to use the motion narrative. We therefore use the structured reasoning prompt for all downstream experiments. A.5 Cross-modal training and inference: Implementation Details The motion encoder fmot is 2-layer, 4-head Transformer with hidden dimension 256 and maximum sequence length 1024. All projection MLPs map into shared 512-dimensional retrieval space via LayerNorm Linear GELU Dropout(0.1) Linear, with L2-normalized outputs. The contrastive temperature is initialized at τ =0.07 and learned during training. We train with AdamW (lr=5105, weight decay 104, gradient clipping 1.0, constant schedule), batch size 512, for up to 40 epochs with early stopping (patience 10). The model is first warmed up on the transcriptmotion objective LTM alone. For the full objective (Eq. 2), we set λs=0.15, λp=0.03, and λb=0.02. All models are trained on single H100 GPU. Comparison Approaches. All baseline retrieval models are evaluated using identical data splits, motion encoders, projection heads, batch sizes, and evaluation protocols unless otherwise noted. To ensure fair comparison, our plain text-motion baseline utilizes the exact same retrieval backbone but is trained exclusively with the standard transcript-motion contrastive objective Ltm, omitting the semantic motion anchor framework entirely. GestureDiffuCLIP replaces our Qwen3-Embedding-8B text encoder with frozen CLIP ViT-B/32 (512-dim, no instruction prompts) and trains with plain symmetric InfoNCE loss between transcripts and motion, with no negative-handling or anchor supervision. TMR uses the same Qwen3-Embedding-8B encoder as ours but trains with transcriptmotion InfoNCE augmented by false-negative filtering, masking within-batch pairs whose transcript cosine similarity exceeds 0.9 following [Petrovich et al., 2023], JEGAL (text-only) also uses Qwen and replaces hard negatives with soft positive targets: pairs with transcript cosine similarity above 0.85 are assigned partial positive weight of 0.5, adapting JEGALs global phrase contrastive objective to our setting. The only varying factor across methods is the training objective, making the comparison direct test of loss design. A.6 Marginal Sensitivity of Auxiliary Loss Weights Figure 4 complements the joint sensitivity heatmap in Figure 2 by showing how mean MRR varies when each auxiliary weight is swept independently while the other is held fixed. Specifically, λp is fixed at 0.03 for the transcript-intent sweep, and λs is fixed at 0.15 for the motion-physical sweep, both corresponding to the near-optimal region identified in the joint analysis. Figure 4: Marginal sensitivity of auxiliary loss weights on mean MRR (%) (text -> Motion and Motion -> Text). The two sweeps together reinforce the asymmetric behaviour observed in the joint heatmap. The transcript-intent branch tolerates moderate range of λs values without strong degradation, whereas even small increases in λp beyond the near-zero optimum consistently hurt retrieval performance. This further supports the recommendation to keep λp small while allowing λs to have more flexibility. 18 In addition, Figure 5 extends this analysis by sweeping the bridge loss weight, λb, while holding the other auxiliary weights fixed at their near-optimal values (λp = 0.03 and λs = 0.15). The results demonstrate that the bridge loss weight should be kept very small, with performance peaking at λb = 0.02. The primary role of Lbr is to regularize the shared anchor space at low weight, preventing the physical-form and communicative-intent representations from drifting apart. Increasing λb beyond this point forces these representations to become overly constrained, which actively degrades overall retrieval performance. Figure 5: Sensitivity of mean MRR (%) to the bridge loss weight (λb), with λp and λs fixed. A.7 Decomposing the Semantic motion anchor into Physical-Form and Intent Components The unified semantic motion anchor produced by the structured reasoning prompt encodes both gesture form and communicative intent in single 12 sentence description. To enable modalitymatched contrastive supervision, we decompose each anchor into its physical-form component aphys and communicative-intent component aint via separate zero-shot prompt to Qwen3-8B that emits JSON object with two fields. The decomposition is run offline once over the full training set of the BEAT2 dataset. Decoding is greedy with fixed token budget of 160; the system and user prompts are reproduced verbatim below. System prompt You split gesture descriptions into exactly two aspects. Return JSON with exactly these keys: physical_motion semantic_intent Rules physical_motion: only the visible body movement, hand shape, position, direction, timing, posture, and spatial form. semantic_intent: only the communicative meaning, emphasis, discourse function, emotion, or concept conveyed by the gesture. Keep both fields concise but faithful. Do not repeat the same content in both fields. If semantic meaning is unclear, set semantic_intent to an empty string. Output valid JSON only. No markdown. User prompt template this gesture description into physical motion and semantic intent. Split {"physical_motion":"...","semantic_intent":"..."}. Return only JSON object like Description: <unified anchor> The two fields are subsequently embedded independently by the frozen Qwen3-Embedding-8B text encoder gtext and projected through the shared anchor projector πan to obtain zp and zs. 19 A.8 Random Anchor Baseline: Implementation Details In the Random Anchor baseline, the text encoder outputs gtext(aphys ) are each replaced by fixed random unit vector of the same dimensionality (4096-d, matching Qwen3-Embedding-8B). For each training sample, each vector is generated deterministically by seeding Gaussian sampler with SHA-256 hash of the sample identifier and L2-normalizing the result; the same vector is reused without modification across all training epochs. The replacement occurs at the text encoder output: the random vectors are fed directly into the anchor projector πan, which is trained normally. This setup tests whether the structural regularization induced by an auxiliary contrastive objective, independent of any linguistic content, is sufficient to explain the gains observed under semantic motion anchor supervision. ) and gtext(aint All three compared methods effectively perform text-to-text retrieval at inference, since the proxy is description, but they differ in how the query and proxy are embedded. Text Contrastive has no anchor projector and routes both query and proxy through its transcript encoder into single transcript-only space an easier setup that requires no cross-projector alignment. Our model and Random Anchor instead route the query through the transcript projector and the proxy through the anchor projector πan, matching them in the learned shared space stricter test of whether anchor supervision builds transferable languagegesture representation. Random Anchor is itself strong control: on BEAT2, it outperformed Text Contrastive  (Table 2)  by adding structured though semantically uninformative auxiliary signal that regularized the shared space; carrying it forward here tests whether that regularization effect alone suffices for cross-domain transfer, or whether the semantic content of the anchors is what matters. A.9 Cross-Dataset Proxy Retrieval Metrics Because TED and BEAT2 do not provide exact cross-dataset paired retrieval targets, we evaluate ranked results using two proxy relevance signals: continuous semantic-context similarity score and discrete exact semantic-label match. Let query be denoted by q, and let the retrieved gallery items ranked by model be (g1, g2, . . . , gK). Semantic-context relevance. For each query q, we associate semantic reference text s(q) and for each gallery item we associate gallery-side semantic reference t(g). Both texts are embedded using frozen Qwen3 text embedding model, yielding normalized vectors ϕ(s(q)) and ϕ(t(g)). We define the graded semantic relevance of gallery item to query as r(q, g) = max(cid:0)0, cos(cid:0)ϕ(s(q)), ϕ(t(g))(cid:1)(cid:1) . The win rate is WinRate(m) = 1 (cid:88) qQ I[mprop(q) > mbase(q)] . (25) Analogously, loss rate and tie rate are computed using < and = comparisons, respectively. A.10 Additional Results: Rank distribution Figure 6 plots the cumulative fraction of queries for which the ground-truth motion is retrieved among the top-k candidates, across all rank cutoffs k. The proposed semantic-anchored model consistently yields higher cumulative fraction than the direct text-motion baseline. Crucially, this advantage is heavily concentrated in the low-rank regime, precisely where co-speech gesture system must commit to single retrieval decision. While both models eventually recover most queries at higher ranks, the consistent gap at low indicates that semantic motion anchor supervision improves the overall ranking structure, not merely isolated operating points such as R@1. Figure 6: Cumulative distribution of ground-truth ranks for text-to-motion retrieval. higher curve indicates that more queries retrieve their paired groundtruth motion at lower ranks. The proposed semanticanchored model consistently outperforms the textmotion baseline, with the largest gap in the low-rank regime, which dictates operational retrieval quality. Here, the motion description is passed via the transcript encoder. A.12 Additional Results: Full Table of TED-to-TED A.13 Details on Perceptual User Study We conducted user study with 32 anonymous participants, primarily comprising university academic staff and students, using an online evaluation form. Each participant was presented with 10 forced-choice questions, where each question displayed side-by-side gestural animation comparing gestures retrieved by our method against those retrieved by RAG-Gestures LLM-based gesture type retrieval approach. For this evaluation, we utilized the same input word query which Mughal et al. [2025] retrieved as the query word. We then replace their word-to-motion retrieval framework with our anchor-based motion retrieval for given word. For every pairwise comparison, participants answered the following question: Which of the two gestures better suits the red highlighted word written above? Figure 7 shows screenshot of the evaluation interface used in our study. Figure 7: Screen Capture of User Study Interface. The query input word is highlighted in red. 23 Example 1 Self-reference ". . . think my favourite thing about Halloween is the haunted houses, Im big fan of the rush of feeling scared. . . GT: One hand rises to chest level and settles near the . . . , marking personal emphasis. It conveys the speakers own enthusiasm and fondness for the thrill of haunted houses. Ours: Both hands lift slightly from below the waist and hover . . . . This conveys heightened positive emotion, emphasizing how happy and excited the speaker felt. Text & Random: Both hands rise to chest level and spread outward, then settle lower and apart with open palms, as if laying out broad area. This depicts something being widely present . . . just one time period. Example 2 Other-reference ". . . went back to the hotel that we were staying at, Im back to where last saw them. . . " GT: One hand extends outward and then lowers . . . , as if marking location in space, to indicate returning to the place where the speaker last saw them. Ours: One hand points outward at chest level . . . specific place or person being referred to in the story. Text & Random: Both hands . . . , as if presenting the situation in front of the speaker. The gesture conveys an offering or reference to the bad circumstances happening to other people. Example 3 Other-reference ". . . and then girl think she was in like university or something. . . " GT: One hand is . . . in small presenting gesture, as if introducing or referring to the girl being mentioned. Ours: One hand extends slightly forward . . . , as if indicating person positioned ahead. It serves to point out the girl sitting in front within the described scene. Text: Both hands spread slightly outward . . . , palm-up presentation, conveying the idea of limited number of social festivals and presenting this as general observation. Random: One hand lifts and makes small . . . , as if marking off another item. It serves to single out "tennis" as an additional example in the list. Example 4 Symbolise ". . . it uses very long weapon, like big big big sword. . . " GT: One hand sweeps outward and rises . . . level, briefly pointing as it extends to depict the great length and imposing size of the imagined sword. Ours: Both hands are held apart in front of the body, . . . , with open palms, to convey the largeness or broad significance of what is being described. Text & Random: Both hands . . . turned out, marking contrast. The gesture helps separate and dismiss "the job" itself as not being the source of the problem. Example 5 Symbolise ". . . its amazing because its huge, dont know how many people have the day off at the same time. . . " GT: Both hands sweep upward and outward . . . and lower outward with open palms, to depict the great size and expansiveness of the place. Ours: Both hands move outward and down from . . . , then hold wide apart. The gesture presents broad set of possibilities or "all these answers," emphasizing their collective scope. Text & Random: Both . . . , with open palms facing outward, to negate or dismiss the idea of separate "real life." Example 7 Enumeration ". . . Ill actually give each restaurant score based on how good the food is. . . " GT: Both hands rise briefly to chest level . . . slightly more active as if marking off items. The gesture conveys the speakers intention to assign or enumerate score for each restaurant. Ours: Both hands lower and hold forward . . . , as if laying out set of rules. This presents the category of acceptable versus unacceptable behavior and underscores clear boundaries. Text: One hand lifts . . . slightly toward the speaker, while the other remains low and still, to refer to spending time with oneself in personal, inviting way. [Self-reference] Random: One hand lifts and moves . . . , as if casually indicating usual destination. It conveys matter-of-fact reference to the speakers routine of going to the library. Example 8 Symbolise ". . . in the middle of my room there is big soft bed. . . " GT: Both hands rise . . . in broad, relaxed spread as if laying out space. The gesture depicts the beds large, expansive presence in the room. Ours: Both hands . . . and spread down toward the waist. The gesture depicts laying out or displaying the pictures on the wall. Text: Both hands . . . , with one hand shifting palm-up to the side, to indicate the other side of the room and present the small desk located there. Random: Both hands move down and slightly . . . , held open as if presenting spread of items. This depicts the accumulation or abundance of clothes, bags, and shoes.
